# **Operative Ekphrasis and the Emergence of Worldtext: Reclassifying Multimodal Generation from Image to World Model**

## **The Operative Turn and the Obsolescence of the Imagetext**

The ancient ekphrastic question—can words make an audience see?—has been definitively answered by the advent of generative artificial intelligence, yet in the very moment of its technological fulfillment, that answer has already been rendered obsolete. The decisive theoretical intervention required today is not merely to document or celebrate the collapse of the word-to-image distinction, a phenomenon Hannes Bajohr accurately diagnoses as the "operative turn".1 Rather, the necessary critical move is to demonstrate that contemporary image generation is merely a way-station toward world generation. The generative prompt is evolving; it no longer stops at pointing toward a static visual representation but increasingly constitutes the operating conditions of a navigable environment. The fundamental shift in digital media theory and computer science is the transition from word-to-image to word-to-world-model.

For the better part of the twentieth century, the dominant theoretical framework for ekphrasis was governed by a profound and deliberate narrowing of the concept. As established by Leo Spitzer's influential 1955 analysis of John Keats's "Ode on a Grecian Urn," ekphrasis was strictly redefined and constrained as the "poetic description of a pictorial or sculptural work of art".3 This formalist constraint, later codified by theorists like James A. W. Heffernan as the "verbal representation of visual representation," effectively concealed the original operativity of the ancient rhetorical practice.3 By restricting ekphrasis to the verbal representation of a static visual artifact, twentieth-century literary criticism forced the concept into a rigid medial boundary, emphasizing the friction, intermedial tension, and ultimate impossibility of perfectly translating visuality into text.6 This era of scholarship produced rich analyses of literary pictorialism and spatial form, yet it fundamentally obscured the productive, world-building capacity of the word.5

However, as classical scholars and rhetoricians have demonstrated, the ancient Greek definition of ekphrasis, rooted in the *progymnasmata* (the foundational rhetorical exercises of antiquity), did not require a work of art as its subject.6 Ancient ekphrasis was defined not by its object, but by its function—*enargeia*, or vividness—and its operative capacity to bring a subject, whether a sprawling battle, a complex person, or a geographical place, before the eyes of the audience.6 It was fundamentally a technology of production, not merely of representation.

The digital age catalyzed a gradual return to this performative understanding long before the release of modern large language models. As Renate Brosch argues in her foundational work on digital ekphrasis, the practice operates as a "performative strategy" that transforms language into something dynamically experienced and inhabitable, heavily reliant on cultural memory and the rapid cognitive processing of abbreviated visual cues.10 In digital culture, ekphrasis moves outside the museum and the gallery, engaging in a transmedial dialogue where images are understood as constructed, non-natural artifacts.13 In the context of multimodal artificial intelligence, the description of a visual object is no longer a rhetorical illusion but a literal, computational execution.

W.J.T. Mitchell’s concept of the *imagetext*—the theoretical compound naming the inextricable intertwining of the verbal and the visual—has long served as the analytical horizon for critical media theory. The imagetext was the goal and the nightmare of the ekphrastic tradition: the theoretical point where the distinction between text and image breaks down. Today, multimodal neural networks have fully operationalized the imagetext. As Bajohr notes, AI systems based on stochastic machine learning treat the text/image collapse not as a metaphor but as a structural reality; in the multidimensional latent space of a diffusion model, words and images are mapped into the exact same mathematical vectors.2

![][image1]

Yet, the achievement of the operative imagetext marks a threshold, not a destination. Generative AI is moving rapidly beyond the production of static, two-dimensional matrices of pixels. The true paradigm shift occurs when description ceases to generate an image and begins to specify a *world model*. The transition from the imagetext to what must now be termed the *worldtext* requires a fundamental reclassification of generative AI: the technology is ceasing to be an image-maker and is becoming a world-model operator. The term *worldtext* names the exact moment when text description becomes an interface to a systemic, spatiotemporal prediction engine.

The foundational architecture for this shift was established by David Ha and Jürgen Schmidhuber in their 2018 framework for "World Models".17 A world model is defined as an internal mental representation of an environment, trained to learn compressed spatial and temporal representations in an unsupervised manner.17 Crucially, Ha and Schmidhuber define this not as a generator of static visual artifacts, but as a generative neural network capable of predicting the future states of a reinforcement learning environment.17 When a text prompt interfaces with an architecture designed to predict spatiotemporal dynamics, the text does not caption a picture; it initializes a complex state. The resulting visual output is merely the surface manifestation of a deeper, mathematically sustained environmental simulation.

## **Platform Realism and the Architecture of the Latent Space**

To comprehend the operative mechanics of the worldtext, one must first analyze the environment in which generative text operates. Contemporary multimodal models do not "see" the world in any optical or human sense; rather, they engage in what media scholars describe as "platform seeing".19 Generative AI systems interact with the world entirely through the processing of invisual data.19 As Roland Meyer points out in his analysis of platform realism, the operative space of these models is composed of invisible flows of contextual data, API connections, and the blind statistical processing of billions of pre-existing image-text pairs scraped from platforms like Wikipedia, Flickr, and Shopify.19 The visible world enters this latent space not as a direct simulation or photographic registration, but as a faint, mathematically distorted echo mediated by massive, structurally biased training datasets.19

The aesthetics produced by these systems are governed by a paradigm Meyer terms "platform realism".19 Platform realism is a second-order aesthetic of generic images that are optimized not to reflect physical reality, but to match textual descriptions, corporate standards, and consumer expectations.19 This aesthetic logic fundamentally privileges the "sayable" over the "visible".19 Within the multidimensional statistical feature space of a generative model, words and images are inextricably linked, meaning that the visual output is entirely dependent on what can be formulated in text.19 Prompts function not as exact executable programming languages, but as fuzzy parameters or search commands that steer the AI toward specific, highly probable regions of statistical space.19

| Dimensions of Platform Realism | Operational Mechanism | Aesthetic Outcome |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Legibility** | Primacy of language; matching visual output strictly to textual prompts. | Images function as literal visualizations of the "sayable." |
| **Plausibility** | Statistical optimization for spatial consistency and physical integrity. | Visual coherence that avoids algorithmic hallucinations (e.g., anatomical errors). |
| **Familiarity** | Remediation of established media forms and historical "vibes." | Structural nostalgia; outputs resemble stock photography or high-end game rendering. |

The operative space of images is continually refined through an endless recursive feedback loop of human interaction.19 Models are optimized based on the algorithmization of taste: human reactions, quantified as likes, shares, ratings, and upscales, are fed back into the systems to train them to better anticipate the viewer's gaze.19 Consequently, AI-generated images are structurally nostalgic; they are expected to look like established media forms—such as traditional photography, digital stock imagery, or cinematic renderings—while maintaining a plausibility that satisfies the broadest possible audience.19 This results in outputs that are, by definition, highly generic, often reproducing social hierarchies and tokenized forms of diversity embedded within the original training data.21

Furthermore, the logic of the prompt is rarely an unmediated transmission of user intent. Platforms enforce corporate aesthetic and safety standards through mechanisms like "shadow prompting" or prompt transformation.19 In systems like DALL-E 3, a user's simple prompt is intercepted and internally expanded by a large language model to include specific environmental details or diversity requirements before being sent to the image generator.19 This creates an aesthetic path dependency, establishing a narrow channel through which creativity flows, heavily contoured by the ideological and technical constraints naturalized by the platform's interface.22 In this environment, simulation ceases to function as imitation and becomes a mode of ontological production, where the model predicts the specific conditions under which something will appear real or trustworthy to the user.19

## **Prompting as Performed Thick Description**

Understanding the constraints of platform realism fundamentally changes how one must approach the practice of "prompt engineering." Prompting is frequently, and erroneously, conceptualized as a straightforward, transactional act of description—a command issued to a neutral machine that dutifully renders the requested output.24 This "vending machine" model of human-computer interaction mischaracterizes both the underlying technology and the socio-technical practice itself. Instead, prompting must be understood as an iterative performance against a model that is already deeply saturated with cultural codes, captions, genres, styles, and visual clichés.

Because the generative model is already "thick" with pre-existing semantic associations and biases, interacting with it requires a methodology akin to what anthropologist Clifford Geertz famously termed "thick description".25 In traditional ethnography, thick description refers to the detailed observation of human behavior that accounts for the complex, layered structures of cultural meaning—for example, recognizing the vast semiotic difference between an involuntary eye twitch and a culturally encoded, conspiratorial wink.25 Applied to generative artificial intelligence, thick description ceases to be a qualitative research method utilized solely by social scientists and becomes an operational necessity for the end-user.27

Recent research into the alignment of Large Language Models has demonstrated that relying predominantly on quantitative benchmarks or superficial demographic proxies fails to capture the deeply nuanced nature of human cultures.27 As Orlowski et al. (2025) argue, AI systems must produce "thick outputs" that reflect deeper cultural meanings grounded firmly in user-provided context and intent.27 Similarly, in a 12-week ethnographic study of software development teams building pedagogical chatbots, researchers found that evaluating LLMs requires a situated understanding of how values, constraints, and professional expertise are negotiated through daily practice, proving that the prompt and its evaluation are heavily overdetermined by cultural context.25

When a user provides a "thin" prompt—such as the simple command, "Make Achilles' shield"—the model automatically defaults to its highest-probability pathways. It yields to the gravitational pull of platform realism. Under a thin prompt, the model acts autonomously, filling the vacuum of user intent with its own statistical averages, resulting in an output that is highly generic and detached from specific cultural or narrative meaning.23

| Prompting Paradigm | Conceptual Model | User Action | System Output | Ekphrastic Mode |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Thin Prompting** | Vending Machine | Direct Command ("Caption") | Generic, Probabilistic Average | Representation (Imagetext) |
| **Thick Prompting** | Cultivation / Loop | Iterative Performance ("Score") | Situated, Contextually Grounded | Constitution (Worldtext) |

To overcome this generic algorithmic gravity, the user must engage in *thick prompting*. Thick prompting is not synonymous with mere verbosity or the thoughtless accumulation of adjectives. It is the strategic, selective application of situated pressure. A thick prompt for the shield ("Make the shield as a worked object, scene by scene, with sky, city, field, dance, war, law, harvest, and ocean as a living order") provides the system with sufficient contextual constraint to force it out of its default habits and into a specific, highly intentional region of the feature space. The thick prompt functions less like a descriptive caption and more like a musical score or a theatrical script, negotiating with the latent space to cultivate a specific operational state.24

Crucially, the operative unit of this interaction is not the singular prompt, but the continuous loop.8 The process involves writing, generating, reading the system’s output (and analyzing its inevitable misinterpretations), revising the text, and regenerating.24 Within this thick, iterative loop, the generated image loses its status as a final, definitive product. The image becomes *feedback*. It serves as a visual readout of how the mathematical model has interpreted the text's intersection with its training data.25 The user reads this feedback, identifies the model's reliance on specific tropes or its failure to grasp a spatial relationship, and thickens the prompt accordingly. This cyclical process exposes the reality of AI-generated media: it is a co-authored performance where the human user provides the limit and the machine provides the statistical volume.24

## **Homer's Shield of Achilles: The First World-Specification**

To fully comprehend the computational shift from image models to world models, one must look not to the history of modern photography or Renaissance painting, but to the origins of Western literature. Homer’s description of the Shield of Achilles, forged by the god Hephaestus in Book 18 of the *Iliad*, is universally recognized as the foundational text of the ekphrastic tradition.30 However, traditional literary criticism has consistently miscategorized the shield by treating it as a complex picture or an impossible decorative artifact. When viewed through the lens of modern multimodal AI architecture, it becomes clear that Homer’s shield is not an image at all. It is the first conceptual iteration of a world model.

The shield does not depict a frozen scene; it specifies a totalizing, operational world. As classical scholar Oliver Taplin (1980) and later Richard Seaford have demonstrated through rigorous structural analysis, the shield is a carefully bounded cosmological model that prefigures the emerging Greek *polis*.32 Hephaestus begins the creation by forging the macroscopic parameters of the environment: "earth and sky and sea" and the "heavenly bodies," including the sun, moon, and specific constellations like the Pleiades and Orion.32 This is not a static painted backdrop; it is the establishment of a systematic, impersonal cosmology—effectively a physics engine and a geographical coordinate system.32

From this cosmic baseline, the shield's description zooms into detailed micro-environments structured by what Seaford terms "nested oppositions" and "balanced conflicts".32 The interior zones specify human activities through a series of symmetrical structures: a city at peace contrasted with a city at war, agricultural labor juxtaposed with communal dance, and legal proceedings.31 These scenes are characterized by duration, procedure, and state-changes.

The trial scene in the peaceful city, for example, features two men quarreling over compensation for a killing.32 This is not a static tableau but a procedural algorithm for resolving conflict. The conflict is described using identical metrical and syntactical forms, creating a balanced, undecided "antithesis" that establishes the limits (πεῖραρ) of behavior necessary for peaceful resolution within society.32 Similarly, the city at war depicts two opposed armies led by Ares and Athena, but crucially, no outcome or victory is stated for either side, reinforcing a continuous state of balanced opposition.32 Finally, the entire construct is bounded by the river Ocean (Okeanos) running along the outermost rim of the shield.32 The Ocean serves as the absolute geographical and conceptual perimeter—the boundary beyond which the world model possesses no data.

![][image2]

This structural reading maps with astonishing precision onto the technical definition of computational world models established by Ha and Schmidhuber.17 In their 2018 architecture, an artificial agent navigates an environment not by reacting to raw pixels, but by learning a compressed representation of that environment. The system is comprised of three core components:

1. **The Vision (V) Model:** A Variational Autoencoder that compresses high-dimensional observations (the visual "surface" of the world) into a compact latent vector (![][image3]), capturing essential abstract features while discarding noise.17  
2. **The Memory (M) Model:** A Mixture Density Network-Recurrent Neural Network (MDN-RNN) that acts as a predictive engine. It analyzes past actions and states to output a probability density function (![][image4] modeled as a mixture of Gaussian distributions) of future states, effectively simulating the flow of time and physics.17  
3. **The Controller (C):** A simple, single-layer linear model trained via Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) that maps the current vision and memory states directly to an action (![][image5]).17

Homer’s shield anticipates this exact tripartite architecture. The visual descriptions of the metalwork, introduced in aggregative, list-like language, parallel the Vision (V) model, providing the sensory surface.32 The procedural algorithms of the harvest, the battle, and the trial function as the Memory (M) model, predicting how the environment changes over time in response to human actions. The warrior who carries the shield, or the audience who visualizes it, functions as the Controller (C), learning to navigate the complexities of mortal life by exploring the "hallucinated dream" generated by the artifact, much as Ha and Schmidhuber trained their agents inside a hallucinated version of the Car Racing and VizDoom environments.17

## **Computational Worldtexts: Generative Environments and Spatial Intelligence**

The critical distinction in contemporary AI research is no longer the semantic gap between text and image, but the architectural gap between image generation and world generation. Image models like DALL-E or Midjourney generate *imagetexts*—they ask the latent space, "What can a sentence make visible?".19 They produce flat, static surfaces optimized for plausibility.19 World models, conversely, generate *worldtexts*—they ask the latent space, "What can a sentence make habitable?"

This monumental shift is currently materializing in emergent platforms designed specifically to transform prompts into fully interactive, state-driven environments. Google DeepMind’s Genie series (including Genie 2 and Genie 3\) represents a massive leap in this direction.38 Genie 2 is an 11-billion parameter foundation world model that generates an endless variety of action-controllable, playable 3D environments from a single text or image prompt.38 Trained on a large-scale video dataset, Genie 2 exhibits emergent understanding of physics, object interactions, and complex character animation.38 It maintains visual consistency while simulating the consequences of user actions—such as jumping, swimming, or navigating—and can even model and predict the behavior of other agents within the generated environment.38 When a user prompts Genie 2 with a text description, the model does not render a flat backdrop; it initializes a coordinate space with gravity, collision detection, and navigable depth.

| System Architecture | Primary Function | Output Format | Interaction Mode |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Generative Image Model** (e.g., Midjourney) | Token-to-pixel prediction | Static 2D matrix | Iterative prompting; non-navigable |
| **Foundation World Model** (e.g., Genie 2\) | Spatiotemporal prediction | Dynamic 3D simulation | Action-controllable; keyboard/mouse inputs |
| **Spatial Intelligence Model** (e.g., Marble) | 3D Scene Construction via Gaussian Splats | Persistent 3D environment with exportable meshes | Camera rotation; semantic object modeling |

Similarly, World Labs, spearheaded by AI pioneer Fei-Fei Li, has developed "Marble," a system predicated on "Spatial Intelligence" rather than mere token generation.41 Marble utilizes Gaussian splats to construct highly detailed 3D scenes complete with depth, lighting, geometry data, and exportable collider meshes.41 It transforms a text prompt into a persistent space that can be rotated through a 360-degree visual field and physically navigated.41 Unlike standard image models that only produce pixels, Marble provides an access route to semantic, object-level modeling in 3D space, moving from manual scene design to infinite variation for training data.41 While currently intrinsically static—requiring external tools like Infinigen or RoboSuite for dynamic robotics simulations—it highlights the drive to create physical scaffolding for cognition.41

This pursuit of world-specification extends far beyond gaming and virtual reality. Yann LeCun has consistently argued in his foundational papers that true autonomous machine intelligence requires predictive models of the world, such as his Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), rather than pure pattern matching or LLM-style token prediction.18 His V-JEPA 2 model supports zero-shot robot control in unfamiliar environments by learning interactive physical reasoning.18 In the autonomous vehicle sector, companies are deploying generative world models to simulate realistic driving scenarios. Wayve's GAIA-1 simulates traffic dynamics, while OccWorld builds 3D occupancy models that allow a vehicle to reason about space by predicting how nearby objects will move continuously, rather than frame-by-frame.42

The generative prompt in these systems is moving past the surface. It is no longer sovereign over a blank canvas; it is one operator among many, collaborating with physics engines, system prompts, structural guardrails, and procedural rules to establish an environment. The text becomes the constitution of the simulation. A world model is inherently stateful, layered, and cumulative. Where Homer’s shield was a world carried by a warrior, the computational world model is a shield the user can step inside. The logic of the *worldtext* demands that we treat generative AI not as a decentralized digital printing press, but as an environmental engine.

## **Conclusion: The Threshold Beyond the Image**

Ekphrasis once asked whether words could make an audience see. The operative turn asked what happens when words literally manufacture images. The emergence of the worldtext now poses the ultimate, far-reaching question: what happens when words generate operational worlds?

It is vital to recognize that the identification of the worldtext does not conclude with a triumphalist assertion that perfect, flawless world generation has definitively arrived. Current world models face severe technical limitations. They struggle with long-term memory, temporal drift, and the immense, often prohibitive computational costs of maintaining high-fidelity physics simulations.39 As seen in platforms like World Labs' Marble, systems often excel at spatial construction but lack the native embedded knowledge of "object physics" required to understand that removing a brick from an arch will cause it to collapse.41 Generative world-building remains highly unstable, inextricably bound to corporate platforms, and fundamentally reliant on the vast, heavily biased sediment of human descriptions and invisual data scraped from the internet.19

This theoretical landscape is being rigorously scrutinized by scholars across interdisciplinary networks. Within the field of Critical AI Studies, academic centers at institutions such as Emory University and the Georgia Institute of Technology are actively probing the societal implications of these technologies.44 Researchers like Matthew K. Gold, Joycelyn Wilson, and Yeqing Kong are investigating the intersections of digital humanities, critical making, and responsible AI design, demonstrating that the deployment of these systems must be evaluated through lenses of historical inequity, power imbalances, and cultural diversity.46 Furthermore, scholars like Lucy Suchman warn that AI frequently operates as a "floating signifier," utilizing interpretive flexibility to maximize its suggestive power and secure further investment.50 These critiques emphasize that as AI transitions from image generation to world simulation, the necessity for critical digital literacy and ethical oversight becomes exponentially more urgent.45

Therefore, *worldtext* should not be understood as a finished, totalizing theory, but as a critical threshold concept. It marks the exact coordinate where the operative capacity of language opens the door beyond the two-dimensional image and spills into the multidimensional state space of the world model. We are standing at the threshold of this divergence. The theoretical vocabularies relied upon today—operative ekphrasis, thick prompting, the imagetext, and platform realism—will inevitably splinter and adapt as the underlying technologies evolve from generating discrete visual artifacts to sustaining persistent, interactive realities.

To understand this technological moment requires looking backward as much as forward. The desire to compress the infinite complexity of the cosmos into a bounded, navigable artifact is not an invention of contemporary computer science; it is an ancient ambition forged on Hephaestus's anvil. Generative AI does not introduce a novel human desire; it merely operationalizes one of our oldest literary fantasies. Humanity once asked language to preserve the world through description. Now, it commands language to generate new ones. The computational mechanisms have achieved unprecedented complexity, but the fundamental wish has changed far less than we think.
